ML-KEM (FIPS 203) is vulnerable to side-channel attacks during decapsulation if not constant-time. Timing leakage in NTT operations identified in Kyber (predecessor). Use validated implementations: liboqs, AWS-LC, BouncyCastle PQC. RNG failures catastrophic — use FIPS 140-3 validated DRBG. API misuse risk: do not reuse keypairs across sessions; check ciphertext length before decapsulation.

ML-KEM-768 (NIST recommended level 3). Same side-channel profile as ML-KEM-512. Decapsulation must be constant-time. Reference constant-time implementations: https://github.com/pq-crystals/kyber. Ensure secret key zeroization post-use.

ML-KEM-1024 (Level 5 / AES-256 equivalent security). Same implementation attack surface as ML-KEM-512/768. Preferred for CNSA 2.0 compliance. Use hardware acceleration (AES-NI for NTT) where available.

ML-DSA (FIPS 204) signing is deterministic by default (randomized optional). Side-channel leakage in polynomial arithmetic (NTT/rejection sampling) documented. Fault injection can recover signing key if not protected. Use hedged signing (randomized) for robustness against fault attacks. Validate public key before verification operations.
ML-DSA-65 (Level 3, recommended for general use). Apply same countermeasures as ML-DSA-44. Signing loop (rejection sampling) must be protected against timing leakage.
ML-DSA-87 (Level 5, highest security). Preferred for CNSA 2.0 firmware signing. Same countermeasures as ML-DSA-44/65. Increased signature size (4627 bytes) — validate buffer sizes.

SLH-DSA (FIPS 205) is hash-based (SPHINCS+). No known side-channel attacks on SHA-2/SHAKE inner functions. Fault injection during signing can leak randomness. CRITICAL: Unlike ML-DSA, SLH-DSA uses a secret seed to derive all keys — seed exposure = total compromise. Ensure seed is generated from FIPS 140-3 DRBG. API misuse: do not use same key for both signature and KEM.
SLH-DSA-SHA2-256s (Level 5). Preferred for software/firmware signing per CNSA 2.0. Same protections as SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s. Large signature size (29,792 bytes) — validate all buffers.

FN-DSA (FIPS 206, Falcon). Signing uses floating-point Gaussian sampling — significant side-channel attack surface (timing leakage from FP operations). Hardware implementations require dedicated countermeasures (see IACR 2023/230). Not recommended for software implementations on constrained devices without constant-time FP.
FN-DSA-1024 (Level 5). Same FP Gaussian sampling attack surface as FN-DSA-512. Compact signature size (1330 bytes) makes it attractive for IoT — but hardware security required.

FrodoKEM (Learning With Errors, no ring structure). Side-channel attacks on matrix sampling documented. Constant-time implementation required. Large key/ciphertext sizes (9,720/9,736 bytes) — not for bandwidth-constrained environments. Used as conservative alternative to ML-KEM where NIST selection concerns exist.

BIKE (NIST alternate KEM, Round 4). Code-based scheme. Decoder side-channel vulnerability: bit-flipping decoder leaks weight of error vector. See IACR 2022/473 for timing attack. Constant-time decoder implementation is non-trivial. Not FIPS-standardized; use only where ML-KEM is not applicable.

HQC (NIST selected 2025 as additional KEM). Code-based, quasi-cyclic. Decoding side-channel risk similar to BIKE. Constant-time polynomial multiplication required. Currently draft standard — avoid production use until FIPS publication. Monitor: https://csrc.nist.gov/projects/post-quantum-cryptography
